title: SEGMENT 13 — Beyond antibodies — glycoengineered Fc fusion proteins

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Beyond antibodies — glycoengineered Fc fusion proteins
So far we've talked exclusively about IgG antibodies.
But Fc engineering has spread to a related class of biologics: Fc fusion proteins.

These are designer molecules in which a non-antibody protein — a receptor extracellular domain, a cytokine, an enzyme — is fused to an IgG Fc, giving it a long serum half-life (via FcRn-mediated recycling) and immune-effector functionality.

Some of these drugs are blockbusters: etanercept (a TNF-receptor Fc fusion for autoimmune disease), abatacept (a CTLA4 Fc fusion for rheumatoid arthritis), aflibercept (a VEGF-trap Fc fusion for macular degeneration), and many others.
These Fc fusions carry the Asn297 glycan, because they contain the IgG Fc with all its standard features.
And in principle, the glycoengineering tools developed for antibodies can be applied to Fc fusion proteins — and increasingly are.
If you have an Fc fusion that you want to have strong ADCC against a target — for example, an Fc fusion that engages a tumor antigen and recruits NK cells — you afucosylate it.
If you want one that's anti-inflammatory, you sialylate it.
A particularly elegant example is the engineering of Fc fusions for anti-inflammatory therapy in autoimmune disease.
Recall from Segment 11 that sialylated Fc is anti-inflammatory.
If you make a recombinant Fc fusion that is highly sialylated, you have a molecule that retains the long half-life and tissue penetration of an antibody but actively suppresses immune activation.
This is the strategy behind several next-generation IVIG substitutes.
Companies have engineered Fc multimers — synthetic clusters of Fcs — with controlled sialylation, designed to act as more potent and more defined replacements for plasma-derived IVIG.
Some of these are in clinical trials for indications like ITP and CIDP.
Another direction is silent Fc engineering — making an Fc fusion or antibody that deliberately does not engage Fc receptors.
This is the opposite of glycoengineering for potency: you might want a silent Fc for a drug that's meant to block a target without triggering immune effector functions.
For example, an anti-checkpoint antibody whose mechanism is purely about blockade — you don't want it killing the cells it binds, you want it just occupying the receptor.

Various approaches achieve silence: protein-engineered mutations like the IgG4 hinge stabilization, "LALA" mutations in the lower hinge, or deglycosylation of Asn297 (which destabilizes the CH2 domain and abolishes Fc receptor binding).
Silent Fc design is its own subfield, but it lives on the same axis as glycoengineering — both are about controlling the immune signal the Fc transmits.
The takeaway for this segment is that Fc engineering, including glycoengineering, has applications well beyond conventional antibodies.
Wherever a drug carries an IgG Fc — and that includes a substantial fraction of the modern biologics pipeline — the Asn297 glycan is a design lever.
Glycoengineering is not just a tool for monoclonals; it's a tool for the whole biologics class that uses Fcs.
